questions.text: I believe that being needed by others is one of the most important things in life.
I see my daily routine as something that emerges from my mood and circumstances.
I feel that building a legacy around my own achievements is a meaningful aspiration.
I see myself as the captain of whatever ship I am on.
I feel that personal freedom is the engine that drives real progress.
I find that separating my emotional reactions from my decision-making process leads to outcomes I am more confident in.
I find that spending an afternoon helping a friend move is more rewarding than spending it on my own pursuits.
I hold the view that the moral concern I feel when I encounter suffering is the quality that keeps my ethical life from becoming mechanical or routine.
I find that my emotional boundaries during others' hardships are what make it possible for me to show up consistently and helpfully.
I experience the feeling of being misunderstood as a sharp, almost physical sting.
I sense when someone uses the word 'interesting' as a placeholder for a reaction they do not want to reveal.
I believe that the moral pull I experience when confronted with suffering is one of the most dependable guides I have for right action.
I design my morning so the most important work gets done before distractions arise.
I notice that my emotional reactions are often more intense than what the situation calls for.
I rarely feel the need to decompress emotionally after social interactions.
I keep careful records of warranties, receipts, and important transactions.
I feel that leading well means ensuring everyone has access to the full picture.
I feel that my own dreams and aspirations are worth fighting for.
I find that I do not need to physically decompress after difficult conversations.
I tend to miss it when someone laughs at the wrong moment because they are uncomfortable.
I do not pick up on when someone places themselves physically in a position of authority.
I find that the intensity of my inner life is something I could never fully convey to someone who doesn't share it.
I frequently reorganize a group's workflow to align with my approach.
I notice that confrontations, while unpleasant, don't leave a physiological residue in my body.
I pick up on the way people rearrange their expressions when they realize they are being observed.
I think that the ability to remain emotionally uninvolved while still acting effectively is one of the most valuable qualities a person can bring to a crisis.
I prefer to be the one who communicates bad news to the team.
I prefer working in environments where roles and expectations are explicit rather than inferred.
I notice when someone's response time to messages changes as a reflection of their shifting feelings.
When I hear about injustice, my first response is indignation and a desire to act, not to analyze.
I regularly accommodate the prevailing direction to maintain group flow.
I hold the view that my life's meaning is directly proportional to my contribution to others.
I take pride in my reputation for dependability and follow-through.
I believe that my unique talents are best expressed through projects I choose for myself.
I look for ways to distribute ownership of problems across the group.
I believe that obsessing over details can prevent you from seeing the bigger picture.
